input.prompt: Return the COMPLETE content of this page exactly as written. Do not summarize, do not omit any sections, do not paraphrase. Return every single word, heading, code block, instruction, and detail from the page in full. Preserve all formatting, structure, and content.

content.content:
# Build Your Open Brain: Complete Setup Guide

This comprehensive guide provides instructions for creating a personal knowledge system that integrates with multiple AI tools through a unified database and retrieval interface.

## System Architecture

The setup consists of two main components:

**Part 1 - Capture:** A Slack channel captures thoughts, which are automatically embedded, classified, and stored in a Supabase database via an Edge Function.

**Part 2 - Retrieval:** An MCP server enables any connected AI tool to search and write to the brain through semantic similarity rather than keyword matching.

## Required Services

All services operate on free tiers:
- **Supabase:** Vector database with search capabilities
- **OpenRouter:** AI gateway providing embeddings and metadata extraction
- **Slack:** Message capture interface

## Cost Structure

For typical usage of 20 thoughts daily, monthly API costs range from approximately $0.10 to $0.30, covering embeddings and metadata extraction services.

## Setup Process

The 13-step installation requires approximately 45 minutes with no coding experience necessary. Users copy and paste configuration code across three services, then deploy Edge Functions to Supabase for message processing and MCP functionality.

## Key Features

The system enables:
- Automatic embedding generation for semantic search
- Metadata extraction identifying people, action items, dates, and topics
- Multi-AI integration (Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor)
- Direct thought capture from any MCP-compatible client
- Persistent cross-tool memory without middleware dependencies

The guide emphasizes saving credentials throughout setup and provides troubleshooting guidance for common issues related to Slack connectivity, function deployment, and AI client configuration.

content.text: The tool summarized the content instead of returning it in full. Let me try again with a more explicit prompt to get the complete text.

input.prompt: I need a verbatim reproduction of this entire page. Output every single line of text from the page. Include all step numbers, all code blocks, all URLs, all configuration snippets, all SQL, all instructions, all notes, all warnings. Do not skip anything. Do not summarize. The user needs to follow these instructions step by step so every detail matters. Start from the very top of the page and go to the very bottom.

content.content:
# Build Your Open Brain: Complete Setup Guide

This guide walks you through creating a personal knowledge system with semantic search across three free services: Supabase (database), OpenRouter (AI gateway), and Slack (capture interface).

## Overview

The system has two parts:

**Part 1 — Capture**: Messages typed in Slack automatically get embedded and classified, then stored in your database with confirmation replies.

**Part 2 — Retrieval**: An MCP server lets Claude, ChatGPT, and other AI tools search your brain by meaning and write to it directly.

## What You Need

- About 45 minutes
- Zero coding experience
- Three free-tier accounts (Slack, Supabase, OpenRouter)
- A terminal (Mac: Terminal app; Windows: PowerShell)

## Cost

For 20 thoughts daily: roughly $0.10–0.30/month in API costs (embeddings and metadata extraction).

## Key Steps Summary

**Part 1 creates the capture pipeline**: Supabase project → database schema → OpenRouter API key → Slack app → Edge Function deployment → Slack event subscription → test message.

**Part 2 creates the retrieval system**: Generate access key → deploy MCP server → connect to Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, or other MCP clients.

## Critical Advice

Before starting, copy the credential tracker template into a text editor and fill it as you progress. You'll need API keys, tokens, and IDs at specific moments—sometimes minutes after creation, sometimes much later. The guide includes detailed troubleshooting for common issues like webhook verification failures, duplicate database entries, and connection problems.

The guide emphasizes that the Supabase built-in AI assistant (available in every project dashboard) can help debug Supabase-specific problems if you get stuck.

content.text: The tool is still summarizing. Let me try a different approach -- I'll use curl to fetch the raw content directly.

input.description: Fetch raw HTML content from the URL

content.content: Output too large (138.5KB). Full output saved to: /home/ketan/.claude/projects/-home-ketan-project-flotsam/ead9159d-a162-44af-b035-6b28d63874ab/tool-results/btavamfz7.txt Preview (first 2KB): Build Your Open Brain Complete Setup Guide — Guides

content.text: It's HTML. Let me extract the text content properly.

input.command:
curl -sL 'https://promptkit.natebjones.com/20260224_uq1_guide_main' | python3 -c "
import sys, html, re

content = sys.stdin.read()

# Remove script and style tags
content = re.sub(r'<script[^>]*>.*?</script>', '', content, flags=re.DOTALL)
content = re.sub(r'<style[^>]*>.*?</style>', '', content, flags=re.DOTALL)

# Remove HTML tags but preserve structure
content = re.sub(r'<br\s*/?>', '\n', content)
content = re.sub(r'</(p|div|h[1-6]|li|tr|blockquote)>', '\n', content)
content = re.sub(r'<(p|div|h[1-6]|li|tr|blockquote)[^>]*>', '\n', content)
content = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', content)

# Decode HTML entities
content = html.unescape(content)

# Clean up whitespace
lines = content.split('\n')
cleaned = []
for line in lines:
    line = line.strip()
    if line:
        cleaned.append(line)

print('\n'.join(cleaned))
" > /tmp/brain_guide.txt 2>&1
wc -l /tmp/brain_guide.txt
input.description: Extract text content from the HTML page

content.content:
1→Build Your Open Brain Complete Setup Guide — Guides
     2→Guide
     3→Build Your Open Brain
     4→Complete Setup Guide
     5→The infrastructure layer for your thinking. One database, one AI gateway, one chat channel. Any AI you use can plug in. No middleware, no SaaS chains, no Zapier.
     6→This isn't a notes app. It's a database with vector search and an open protocol — built so that every AI tool you use shares the same persistent memory of you. Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Claude Code, whatever ships next month. One brain. All of them.
     7→Before you start: If you hit a wall during setup, you're not on your own. We built a FAQ that covers the most common questions and gotchas people run into. And if you need real-time help, we created dedicated AI assistants that know this system inside and out, one for each major platform: a Claude Skill, a ChatGPT Custom GPT, and a Gemini GEM. They can walk you through any step, troubleshoot connection issues, and answer questions specific to your setup. Use whichever one matches the AI tool you already use.
     8→What You're Building
     9→A Slack channel where you type a thought — it automatically gets embedded, classified, and stored in your database — you get a confirmation reply showing what was captured. Then an MCP server that lets any AI assistant search your brain by meaning — and write to it directly.
    10→What You Need
    11→About 45 minutes and zero coding experience. You'll copy and paste everything.
    12→Services (All Free Tier)
    13→Supabase — Your database — stores everything
    14→OpenRouter — Your AI gateway — understands everything
    15→Slack — Your capture interface — where you type thoughts
    16→If You Get Stuck
    17→Follow this guide step by step — it's designed to get you through without outside help. But if something goes sideways, Supabase has a free built-in AI assistant in every project dashboard. Look for the chat icon in the bottom-right corner. It has access to all of Supabase's documentation and can help with every Supabase-specific step in this guide.
    18→Things it's good at:
    19→Walking you through where to click when you can't find something in the dashboard
    20→Fixing SQL errors if you paste in the error message
    21→Explaining terminal commands and what their output means
    22→Interpreting Edge Function logs when something isn't working
    23→Explaining Supabase concepts in plain English (what's a Secret key / service role key? what does Row Level Security do?)
    24→It can't see your screen or run commands for you, but if you paste what you're seeing, it can tell you what to do next.
    25→Two Parts
    26→Part 1 — Capture (Steps 1–9): Slack → Edge Function → Supabase. Type a thought, it gets embedded and classified automatically.
    27→Part 2 — Retrieval (Steps 10–13): Hosted MCP Server → Any AI. Connect Claude, ChatGPT, or any MCP client to your brain with a URL. Read and write from any tool.
    28→After You're Done
    29→This guide builds the system. The companion prompt pack — Open Brain: Companion Prompts — makes it useful. It includes prompts for migrating your existing AI memories into the brain, migrating an existing second brain system, discovering use cases specific to your workflow, capture templates that optimize metadata extraction, and a weekly review ritual. Finish the setup first, then grab the prompts.
    30→Cost Breakdown
    31→Service
    32→Cost
    33→Slack
    34→Free
    35→Supabase (free tier)
    36→$0
    37→Embeddings (text-embedding-3-small)
    38→~$0.02 / million tokens
    39→Metadata extraction (gpt-4o-mini)
    40→~$0.15 / million input tokens
    41→For 20 thoughts/day: roughly $0.10–0.30/month in API costs.
    42→Credential Tracker
    43→You're going to generate API keys, passwords, and IDs across three different services. You'll need them at specific steps later — sometimes minutes after you create them, sometimes much later. Don't trust your memory.
    44→⚠️ Copy the block below into a text editor (Notes, TextEdit, Notepad) and fill it in as you go. Each item tells you which step generates it.
    45→View & Copy Code
    46→💡 Seriously — copy that now. You'll thank yourself at Step 7.
    47→Part 1 — Capture
    48→Step 1: Create Your Supabase Project
    49→Supabase is your database. It stores your thoughts as raw text, vector embeddings, and structured metadata. It also gives you a REST API automatically.
    50→Go to supabase.com and sign up (GitHub login is fastest)
    51→Click New Project in the dashboard
    52→Pick your organization (default is fine)
    53→Set Project name: open-brain (or whatever you want)
    54→Generate a strong Database password — paste into credential tracker NOW
    55→Pick the Region closest to you
    56→Click Create new project and wait 1–2 minutes
    57→💡 Grab your Project ref — it's the random string in your dashboard URL: supabase.com/dashboard/project/THIS_PART. Paste it into the tracker.
    58→Step 2: Set Up the Database
    59→Three SQL commands, pasted one at a time. This creates your storage table, your search function, and your security policy.
    60→Enable the Vector Extension
    61→In the left sidebar: Database → Extensions → search for "vector" → flip pgvector ON.
    62→Create the Thoughts Table
    63→In the left sidebar: SQL Editor → New query → paste and Run:
    64→View & Copy Code
    65→Create the Search Function
    66→New query → paste and Run:
    67→View & Copy Code
    68→Lock Down Security
    69→One more new query:
    70→-- Enable Row Level Security
    71→alter table thoughts enable row level security;
    72→-- Service role full access only
    73→create policy "Service role full access"
    74→on thoughts
    75→for all
    76→using (auth.role() = 'service_role');
    77→Quick Verification
    78→Table Editor should show the thoughts table with columns: id, content, embedding, metadata, created_at, updated_at. Database → Functions should show match_thoughts.
    79→Step 3: Save Your Connection Details
    80→In the left sidebar: Settings (gear icon) → API. Copy these into your credential tracker:
    81→Project URL — Listed in the API settings section under "Project URL"
    82→Secret key — Under "API keys," this is the key formerly labeled "Service role key." Same key, new name — click reveal and copy it.
    83→⚠️ Treat the Secret key like a password. Anyone with it has full access to your data. You may also see a "Publishable key" listed — that's the anon key surfaced more prominently in the updated Supabase UI. You don't need it for this setup.
    84→Step 4: Get an OpenRouter API Key
    85→OpenRouter is a universal AI API gateway — one account gives you access to every major model. We're using it for embeddings and lightweight LLM metadata extraction.
    86→Why OpenRouter instead of OpenAI directly? One account, one key, one billing relationship — and it future-proofs you for Claude, Gemini, or any other model later.
    87→Go to openrouter.ai and sign up
    88→Go to openrouter.ai/keys
    89→Click Create Key, name it open-brain
    90→Copy the key into your credential tracker immediately
    91→Add $5 in credits under Credits (lasts months)
    92→Step 5: Create Your Slack Capture Channel
    93→If you don't have a Slack workspace, create one at slack.com (free tier works)
    94→Click the + next to Channels → Create new channel
    95→Name it "capture" (or brain, inbox, whatever feels natural)
    96→Make it Private (recommended — this is personal)
    97→Get the Channel ID: right-click channel → View channel details → scroll to bottom (starts with C)
    98→Paste the Channel ID into your credential tracker
    99→Step 6: Create the Slack App
   100→This is the bridge between Slack and your database.
   101→Create the App
   102→Go to api.slack.com/apps → Create New App → From scratch
   103→App Name: "Open Brain", select your workspace
   104→Click Create App
   105→Set Permissions
   106→Left sidebar → OAuth & Permissions
   107→Scroll to Scopes → Bot Token Scopes
   108→Add: channels:history, groups:history, chat:write
   109→Scroll up → Install to Workspace → Allow
   110→Copy the Bot User OAuth Token (starts with xoxb-) into credential tracker
   111→Add App to Channel
   112→In Slack, open your capture channel and type: /invite @Open Brain
   113→💡 Don't set up Event Subscriptions yet — you need the Edge Function URL first (Step 7).
   114→Step 7: Deploy the Edge Function
   115→This is the brains of the operation. One function receives messages from Slack, generates an embedding, extracts metadata, stores everything in Supabase, and replies with a confirmation.
   116→💡 New to the terminal? The "terminal" is the text-based command line on your computer. On Mac, open the app called Terminal (search for it in Spotlight). On Windows, open PowerShell. Everything below gets typed there, not in your browser.
   117→Install the Supabase CLI
   118→💡 Mac users: If you already have Homebrew installed (you'll know — it's the thing you install with brew), use the first option. Windows users: use Scoop — Supabase recommends it over npm for Windows because it handles PATH and permissions cleanly. Linux or Mac without Homebrew: use npm.
   119→# Mac with Homebrew
   120→brew install supabase/tap/supabase
   121→# Windows with Scoop (recommended)
   122→# Install Scoop first if you don't have it:
   123→Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser
   124→Invoke-RestMethod -Uri https://get.scoop.sh | Invoke-Expression
   125→# Then install Supabase:
   126→scoop bucket add supabase https://github.com/supabase/scoop-bucket.git
   127→scoop install supabase
   128→# Linux or Mac without Homebrew
   129→npm install -g supabase
   130→Verify it worked:
   131→supabase --version
   132→Log In and Link
   133→supabase login
   134→supabase link --project-ref YOUR_PROJECT_REF
   135→Replace YOUR_PROJECT_REF with the project ref from your credential tracker (Step 1).
   136→Create the Function
   137→supabase functions new ingest-thought
   138→Open supabase/functions/ingest-thought/index.ts and replace its entire contents with:
   139→View & Copy Code
   140→Set Your Secrets
   141→supabase secrets set OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your-openrouter-key-here
   142→supabase secrets set SLACK_BOT_TOKEN=xoxb-your-slack-bot-token-here
   143→supabase secrets set SLACK_CAPTURE_CHANNEL=C0your-channel-id-here
   144→💡 SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY are automatically available inside Edge Functions — you don't need to set them.
   145→Deploy
   146→supabase functions deploy ingest-thought --no-verify-jwt
   147→⚠️ Copy the Edge Function URL immediately after deployment! It looks like: https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/ingest-thought
   148→Step 8: Connect Slack to the Edge Function
   149→Go to api.slack.com/apps → select your Open Brain app
   150→Left sidebar → Event Subscriptions → toggle Enable Events ON
   151→Paste your Edge Function URL in the Request URL field
   152→Wait for the green checkmark ✅ Verified
   153→Under Subscribe to bot events, add both: message.channels and message.groups
   154→Click Save Changes (reinstall if prompted)
   155→⚠️ You need both events. Slack treats public and private channels as separate entity types. Public channels fire message.channels, private channels fire message.groups. If you only add one, messages in the other channel type will silently fail — no error, just nothing happens. Add both so you're covered regardless of how your capture channel is configured.
   156→Step 9: Test It
   157→Go to your capture channel in Slack and type:
   158→Sarah mentioned she's thinking about leaving her job to start a consulting business
   159→Wait 5–10 seconds. You should see a threaded reply:
   160→Captured as person_note — career, consulting
   161→People: Sarah
   162→Action items: Check in with Sarah about consulting plans
   163→Then open Supabase dashboard → Table Editor → thoughts. You should see one row with your message, an embedding, and metadata.
   164→💡 If that works, Part 1 is done. You have a working capture system.
   165→Part 2 — Retrieval
   166→A Quick Note on Architecture
   167→MCP servers can run two ways: locally on your computer, or hosted in the cloud.
   168→The local approach means installing Node.js, building a TypeScript project, and running a server process on your machine. Every AI client you connect needs the full path to that server plus your database credentials pasted into a config file. If your laptop is closed, your brain is offline. If you switch computers, you set it up again.
   169→We're not doing that.
   170→Your capture system already runs on Supabase — the Edge Function you deployed in Part 1 handles Slack messages without anything running on your computer. The MCP server works the same way. One more Edge Function, deployed to the same project, reachable from anywhere. Your AI clients connect with a URL. No build steps, no local dependencies, no credentials on your machine.
   171→If you want to run locally — maybe you're a developer who prefers that, or you want to customize beyond what Edge Functions allow — the MCP TypeScript SDK with StdioServerTransport works great. The Supabase docs on deploying MCP servers cover both approaches. Everything below uses hosted.
   172→Step 10: Create an Access Key
   173→Your MCP server will be a public URL. The Supabase project ref in that URL is random enough that nobody will stumble onto it, but let's close the gap entirely. You'll generate a simple access key that the server checks on every request. Takes 30 seconds.
   174→In your terminal, generate a random key:
   175→# Mac/Linux
   176→openssl rand -hex 32
   177→# Windows (PowerShell)
   178→-join ((1..32) | ForEach-Object { '{0:x2}' -f (Get-Random -Maximum 256) })
   179→Copy the output — it'll look something like a3f8b2c1d4e5... (64 characters). Paste it into your credential tracker under MCP Access Key.
   180→Set it as a Supabase secret:
   181→supabase secrets set MCP_ACCESS_KEY=your-generated-key-here
   182→Step 11: Deploy the MCP Server
   183→One Edge Function. Four tools: semantic search, browse recent thoughts, stats, and capture. Same deployment process as the capture function.
   184→Create the Function
   185→supabase functions new open-brain-mcp
   186→Add Dependencies
   187→Create supabase/functions/open-brain-mcp/deno.json:
   188→{
   189→"imports": {
   190→"@hono/mcp": "npm:@hono/mcp@0.1.1",
   191→"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "npm:@modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.24.3",
   192→"hono": "npm:hono@4.9.2",
   193→"zod": "npm:zod@4.1.13",
   194→"@supabase/supabase-js": "npm:@supabase/supabase-js@2.47.10"
   195→}
   196→}
   197→Write the Server
   198→Open supabase/functions/open-brain-mcp/index.ts and replace its entire contents with:
   199→View & Copy Code
   200→Deploy
   201→supabase functions deploy open-brain-mcp --no-verify-jwt
   202→Your MCP server is now live at:
   203→https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp
   204→Replace YOUR_PROJECT_REF with the project ref from your credential tracker (Step 1). Paste the full URL into your credential tracker as the MCP Server URL.
   205→Now build your MCP Connection URL by adding your access key to the end:
   206→https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp?key=your-access-key-from-step-10
   207→Paste this into your credential tracker as the MCP Connection URL. This is what you'll give to AI clients that support remote MCP — one URL, no extra config.
   208→💡 That's it. No npm install, no TypeScript build, no local server to keep running. It's deployed alongside your capture function and runs on Supabase's infrastructure.
   209→Step 12: Connect to Your AI
   210→You need your MCP Connection URL from the credential tracker — the one with ?key= at the end.
   211→Claude Desktop
   212→Open Claude Desktop → Settings → Connectors
   213→Click Add custom connector
   214→Name: Open Brain
   215→Remote MCP server URL: paste your MCP Connection URL (the one ending in ?key=your-access-key)
   216→Click Add
   217→That's it. Start a new conversation, and Claude will have access to your Open Brain tools. You can enable or disable it per conversation via the "+" button → Connectors.
   218→💡 No JSON config files. No Node.js. No terminal. If you had trouble with earlier versions of this guide, this is the fix.
   219→ChatGPT
   220→Requires a paid ChatGPT plan (Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise, or Edu) and works on the web at chatgpt.com. Not available on mobile.
   221→Enable Developer Mode (one-time setup):
   222→Go to chatgpt.com → click your profile icon → Settings
   223→Navigate to Apps & Connectors → Advanced settings
   224→Toggle Developer mode ON
   225→⚠️ Enabling Developer Mode disables ChatGPT's built-in Memory feature. Yes, that's ironic for a brain tool. Your Open Brain replaces that functionality anyway — and it works across every AI, not just ChatGPT.
   226→Add the connector:
   227→In Settings → Apps & Connectors, click Create
   228→Name: Open Brain
   229→Description: Personal knowledge base with semantic search (or whatever you want — this is just for your reference)
   230→MCP endpoint URL: paste your MCP Connection URL (the one ending in ?key=your-access-key)
   231→Authentication: select No Authentication (your access key is embedded in the URL)
   232→Click Create
   233→Using it: Start a new conversation and make sure the Open Brain connector is enabled — check the tools/apps panel at the top of the chat. ChatGPT is less intuitive than Claude at picking the right MCP tool automatically. If it doesn't use your brain on its own, be explicit: "Use the Open Brain search_thoughts tool to find my notes about project planning." After it gets the pattern once or twice in a conversation, it usually picks up the habit.
   234→Claude Code
   235→claude mcp add --transport http open-brain \
   236→https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp \
   237→--header "x-brain-key: your-access-key-from-step-10"
   238→Other Clients (Cursor, VS Code Copilot, Windsurf)
   239→Every MCP client handles remote servers slightly differently. The server accepts your access key two ways — pick whichever your client supports:
   240→Option A: URL with key (easiest). If your client has a field for a remote MCP server URL, paste the full MCP Connection URL including ?key=your-access-key. This works for any client that supports remote MCP without requiring headers.
   241→Option B: mcp-remote bridge. If your client only supports local stdio servers (configured via a JSON config file), use mcp-remote to bridge to the remote server. This requires Node.js installed.
   242→{
   243→"mcpServers": {
   244→"open-brain": {
   245→"command": "npx",
   246→"args": [
   247→"mcp-remote",
   248→"https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp",
   249→"--header",
   250→"x-brain-key:${BRAIN_KEY}"
   251→],
   252→"env": {
   253→"BRAIN_KEY": "your-access-key-from-step-10"
   254→}
   255→}
   256→}
   257→}
   258→⚠️ Note: no space after the colon in x-brain-key:${BRAIN_KEY}. Some clients have a bug where spaces inside args get mangled.
   259→Step 13: Use It
   260→Ask your AI naturally. It picks the right tool automatically:
   261→Prompt
   262→Tool Used
   263→"What did I capture about career changes?"
   264→Semantic search
   265→"What did I capture this week?"
   266→Browse recent
   267→"How many thoughts do I have?"
   268→Stats overview
   269→"Find my notes about the API redesign"
   270→Semantic search
   271→"Show me my recent ideas"
   272→Browse + filter
   273→"Who do I mention most?"
   274→Stats
   275→"Save this: decided to move the launch to March 15 because of the QA blockers"
   276→Capture thought
   277→"Remember that Marcus wants to move to the platform team"
   278→Capture thought
   279→💡 The capture tool means you're not limited to Slack for input. Any MCP-connected AI can write directly to your brain — Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, Claude Code, Cursor. Wherever you're working, you can save a thought without switching apps.
   280→Troubleshooting
   281→If the specific suggestions below don't solve your issue, remember: the Supabase AI assistant (chat icon, bottom-right of your dashboard) can help diagnose problems with anything Supabase-related. Paste the error message and tell it what step you're on.
   282→Capture Issues (Part 1)
   283→Slack says "Request URL not verified"
   284→Your Edge Function isn't deployed or isn't reachable. Run the deploy command again and check the output for errors.
   285→supabase functions deploy ingest-thought --no-verify-jwt
   286→Messages aren't triggering the function
   287→Check Event Subscriptions — make sure both message.channels and message.groups are listed (public channels use the first, private channels use the second — you need both). Verify the app is invited to the channel. Confirm the channel ID in your secrets matches the actual channel.
   288→Slack creates duplicate database entries
   289→Slack retries webhook delivery if it doesn't get a response within 3 seconds. If your Edge Function takes longer than that (embedding + metadata extraction can take 4-5 seconds), Slack sends the event again, and you get two rows. This is a known edge case. The captures are identical, so it doesn't affect search — but if it bothers you, you can delete the duplicate row in the Supabase Table Editor.
   290→Function runs but nothing in the database
   291→Check Edge Function logs: Supabase dashboard → Edge Functions → ingest-thought → Logs. Most likely the OpenRouter key is wrong or has no credits.
   292→supabase secrets list
   293→No confirmation reply in Slack
   294→The bot token might be wrong, or chat:write scope wasn't added. Go to your Slack app → OAuth & Permissions and verify. If you added the scope after installing, you need to reinstall the app.
   295→Metadata extraction seems off
   296→That's normal — the LLM is making its best guess with limited context. The metadata is a convenience layer on top of semantic search, not the primary retrieval mechanism. The embedding handles fuzzy matching regardless.
   297→Retrieval Issues (Part 2)
   298→Claude Desktop tools don't appear
   299→Make sure you added the connector in Settings → Connectors (not by editing the JSON config file). Verify the connector is enabled for your conversation — click the "+" button at the bottom of the chat, then Connectors, and check that Open Brain is toggled on. If the connector was added but tools still don't show, try removing and re-adding it with the same URL.
   300→ChatGPT doesn't use the Open Brain tools
   301→First, confirm Developer Mode is enabled (Settings → Apps & Connectors → Advanced settings). Without it, ChatGPT only exposes limited MCP functionality that won't cover Open Brain's full toolset. Next, check that the connector is active for your current conversation — look for it in the tools/apps panel. If it's connected but ChatGPT ignores it, be direct: "Use the Open Brain search_thoughts tool to search for [topic]." ChatGPT often needs explicit tool references the first few times before it starts picking them up automatically.
   302→Getting 401 errors
   303→The access key doesn't match what's stored in Supabase secrets. Double-check that the ?key= value in your URL matches your MCP Access Key exactly. If you're using the header approach (Claude Code or mcp-remote), the header must be x-brain-key (lowercase, with the dash).
   304→Search returns no results
   305→Make sure you sent test messages in Part 1 first. Try asking the AI to "search with threshold 0.3" for a wider net. If that still returns nothing, check the Edge Function logs in the Supabase dashboard for errors.
   306→Tools work but responses are slow
   307→First search on a cold function takes a few seconds — the Edge Function is waking up. Subsequent calls are faster. If it's consistently slow, check your Supabase project region — pick the one closest to you.
   308→Capture tool saves but metadata is wrong
   309→Same as Slack capture — the metadata extraction is best-effort. The embedding is what powers semantic search, and that works regardless of how the metadata gets classified. If you consistently want a specific classification, use the capture templates from the prompt kit to give the LLM clearer signals.
   310→How It Works Under the Hood
   311→When you type a message in Slack: Slack sends it to your Edge Function → the function generates an embedding (1536-dimensional vector of meaning) AND extracts metadata via LLM in parallel → both get stored as a single row in Supabase → the function replies in your Slack thread with a summary.
   312→When you capture from any AI via MCP: your AI client sends the text to the capture_thought tool → the MCP server generates an embedding AND extracts metadata in parallel (same pipeline as Slack) → stored as a single row → confirmation returned to your AI.
   313→When you ask your AI about it: your AI client sends the query to the MCP Edge Function → the function generates an embedding of your question → Supabase matches it against every stored thought by vector similarity → results come back ranked by meaning, not keywords.
   314→The embedding is what makes retrieval powerful. "Sarah's thinking about leaving" and "What did I note about career changes?" match semantically even though they share zero keywords. The metadata is a bonus layer for structured filtering on top.
   315→Swapping Models Later
   316→Because you're using OpenRouter, you can swap models by editing the model strings in the Edge Function code and redeploying. Browse available models at openrouter.ai/models. Just make sure embedding dimensions match (1536 for the current setup).
   317→What You Just Built — And What You Can Build Next
   318→You just used three free services, some copy-pasted code, and a built-in AI assistant to build a personal knowledge system with semantic search, an open write protocol, and an open read protocol. No CS degree. No local servers. No monthly SaaS fee.
   319→Here's the thing worth noticing: that Supabase AI assistant that helped you through the setup? It has access to all of Supabase's documentation, understands your project structure, and can help you build on top of what you've created. That's not a one-time trick for getting unstuck during setup. That's a permanent building partner.
   320→Want to add a new capture source beyond Slack? Ask it how to create another Edge Function. Want to add a new field to your thoughts table? Ask it to help you write the SQL migration. Want to understand how to add authentication so you can share your brain with a teammate? It knows the docs better than you ever will.
   321→You just built AI infrastructure using AI. That pattern doesn't stop here.
   322→Your Next Step
   323→Your Open Brain is live. Now make it work for you. The companion prompt pack — Open Brain: Companion Prompts — covers the full lifecycle from here:
   324→Memory Migration — Pull everything your AI already knows about you into your brain so every tool starts with context instead of zero
   325→Second Brain Migration — Bring your existing notes from Notion, Obsidian, or any other system into your Open Brain without starting over
   326→Open Brain Spark — Personalized use case discovery based on your actual workflow, not generic examples
   327→Quick Capture Templates — Five patterns optimized for clean metadata extraction so your brain tags and retrieves accurately
   328→The Weekly Review — A Friday ritual that surfaces themes, forgotten action items, and connections you missed
   329→Start with the Memory Migration. If you have an existing second brain, run the Second Brain Migration next. Then use the Spark to figure out what to capture going forward. The templates build the daily habit. The weekly review closes the loop.
   330→Built by Nate B. Jones — companion to "Your Second Brain Is Closed. Your AI Can't Use It. Here's the Fix."
   331→natebjones.com·
   332→Disclaimer
   333→Social
   334→YouTubeTikTokLinkedInSubstack
   335→Apps
   336→AI CredHeyPrestoCourses
   337→Links
   338→

content.text:
Here is the complete content of the guide at `https://promptkit.natebjones.com/20260224_uq1_guide_main`. Note that several code blocks are behind "View & Copy Code" buttons on the page and their contents are not included in the extracted text -- you would need to click those on the live page to see the actual SQL and TypeScript code.

---

# Build Your Open Brain Complete Setup Guide

The infrastructure layer for your thinking. One database, one AI gateway, one chat channel. Any AI you use can plug in. No middleware, no SaaS chains, no Zapier.

This isn't a notes app. It's a database with vector search and an open protocol -- built so that every AI tool you use shares the same persistent memory of you. Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Claude Code, whatever ships next month. One brain. All of them.

**Before you start:** If you hit a wall during setup, you're not on your own. We built a FAQ that covers the most common questions and gotchas people run into. And if you need real-time help, we created dedicated AI assistants that know this system inside and out, one for each major platform: a Claude Skill, a ChatGPT Custom GPT, and a Gemini GEM. They can walk you through any step, troubleshoot connection issues, and answer questions specific to your setup. Use whichever one matches the AI tool you already use.

## What You're Building

A Slack channel where you type a thought -- it automatically gets embedded, classified, and stored in your database -- you get a confirmation reply showing what was captured. Then an MCP server that lets any AI assistant search your brain by meaning -- and write to it directly.

## What You Need

About 45 minutes and zero coding experience. You'll copy and paste everything.

**Services (All Free Tier):**
- **Supabase** -- Your database -- stores everything
- **OpenRouter** -- Your AI gateway -- understands everything
- **Slack** -- Your capture interface -- where you type thoughts

## If You Get Stuck

Follow this guide step by step -- it's designed to get you through without outside help. But if something goes sideways, Supabase has a free built-in AI assistant in every project dashboard. Look for the chat icon in the bottom-right corner. It has access to all of Supabase's documentation and can help with every Supabase-specific step in this guide.

Things it's good at:
- Walking you through where to click when you can't find something in the dashboard
- Fixing SQL errors if you paste in the error message
- Explaining terminal commands and what their output means
- Interpreting Edge Function logs when something isn't working
- Explaining Supabase concepts in plain English (what's a Secret key / service role key? what does Row Level Security do?)

It can't see your screen or run commands for you, but if you paste what you're seeing, it can tell you what to do next.

## Two Parts

**Part 1 -- Capture (Steps 1-9):** Slack -> Edge Function -> Supabase. Type a thought, it gets embedded and classified automatically.

**Part 2 -- Retrieval (Steps 10-13):** Hosted MCP Server -> Any AI. Connect Claude, ChatGPT, or any MCP client to your brain with a URL. Read and write from any tool.

## After You're Done

This guide builds the system. The companion prompt pack -- Open Brain: Companion Prompts -- makes it useful. It includes prompts for migrating your existing AI memories into the brain, migrating an existing second brain system, discovering use cases specific to your workflow, capture templates that optimize metadata extraction, and a weekly review ritual. Finish the setup first, then grab the prompts.

## Cost Breakdown

| Service | Cost |
|---------|------|
| Slack | Free |
| Supabase (free tier) | $0 |
| Embeddings (text-embedding-3-small) | ~$0.02 / million tokens |
| Metadata extraction (gpt-4o-mini) | ~$0.15 / million input tokens |

For 20 thoughts/day: roughly $0.10-0.30/month in API costs.

## Credential Tracker

You're going to generate API keys, passwords, and IDs across three different services. You'll need them at specific steps later -- sometimes minutes after you create them, sometimes much later. Don't trust your memory.

Copy the block below into a text editor (Notes, TextEdit, Notepad) and fill it in as you go. Each item tells you which step generates it.

[View & Copy Code -- credential tracker template]

Seriously -- copy that now. You'll thank yourself at Step 7.

---

# Part 1 -- Capture

## Step 1: Create Your Supabase Project

Supabase is your database. It stores your thoughts as raw text, vector embeddings, and structured metadata. It also gives you a REST API automatically.

1. Go to supabase.com and sign up (GitHub login is fastest)
2. Click New Project in the dashboard
3. Pick your organization (default is fine)
4. Set Project name: open-brain (or whatever you want)
5. Generate a strong Database password -- paste into credential tracker NOW
6. Pick the Region closest to you
7. Click Create new project and wait 1-2 minutes

Grab your Project ref -- it's the random string in your dashboard URL: `supabase.com/dashboard/project/THIS_PART`. Paste it into the tracker.

## Step 2: Set Up the Database

Three SQL commands, pasted one at a time. This creates your storage table, your search function, and your security policy.

**Enable the Vector Extension:**
In the left sidebar: Database -> Extensions -> search for "vector" -> flip pgvector ON.

**Create the Thoughts Table:**
In the left sidebar: SQL Editor -> New query -> paste and Run:

[View & Copy Code -- thoughts table SQL]

**Create the Search Function:**
New query -> paste and Run:

[View & Copy Code -- match_thoughts function SQL]

**Lock Down Security:**
One more new query:

```sql
-- Enable Row Level Security
alter table thoughts enable row level security;

-- Service role full access only
create policy "Service role full access"
on thoughts
for all
using (auth.role() = 'service_role');
```

**Quick Verification:**
Table Editor should show the thoughts table with columns: id, content, embedding, metadata, created_at, updated_at. Database -> Functions should show match_thoughts.

## Step 3: Save Your Connection Details

In the left sidebar: Settings (gear icon) -> API. Copy these into your credential tracker:

- **Project URL** -- Listed in the API settings section under "Project URL"
- **Secret key** -- Under "API keys," this is the key formerly labeled "Service role key." Same key, new name -- click reveal and copy it.

**Warning:** Treat the Secret key like a password. Anyone with it has full access to your data. You may also see a "Publishable key" listed -- that's the anon key surfaced more prominently in the updated Supabase UI. You don't need it for this setup.

## Step 4: Get an OpenRouter API Key

OpenRouter is a universal AI API gateway -- one account gives you access to every major model. We're using it for embeddings and lightweight LLM metadata extraction.

Why OpenRouter instead of OpenAI directly? One account, one key, one billing relationship -- and it future-proofs you for Claude, Gemini, or any other model later.

1. Go to openrouter.ai and sign up
2. Go to openrouter.ai/keys
3. Click Create Key, name it open-brain
4. Copy the key into your credential tracker immediately
5. Add $5 in credits under Credits (lasts months)

## Step 5: Create Your Slack Capture Channel

1. If you don't have a Slack workspace, create one at slack.com (free tier works)
2. Click the + next to Channels -> Create new channel
3. Name it "capture" (or brain, inbox, whatever feels natural)
4. Make it Private (recommended -- this is personal)
5. Get the Channel ID: right-click channel -> View channel details -> scroll to bottom (starts with C)
6. Paste the Channel ID into your credential tracker

## Step 6: Create the Slack App

This is the bridge between Slack and your database.

**Create the App:**
1. Go to api.slack.com/apps -> Create New App -> From scratch
2. App Name: "Open Brain", select your workspace
3. Click Create App

**Set Permissions:**
1. Left sidebar -> OAuth & Permissions
2. Scroll to Scopes -> Bot Token Scopes
3. Add: `channels:history`, `groups:history`, `chat:write`
4. Scroll up -> Install to Workspace -> Allow
5. Copy the Bot User OAuth Token (starts with `xoxb-`) into credential tracker

**Add App to Channel:**
In Slack, open your capture channel and type: `/invite @Open Brain`

Don't set up Event Subscriptions yet -- you need the Edge Function URL first (Step 7).

## Step 7: Deploy the Edge Function

This is the brains of the operation. One function receives messages from Slack, generates an embedding, extracts metadata, stores everything in Supabase, and replies with a confirmation.

**New to the terminal?** The "terminal" is the text-based command line on your computer. On Mac, open the app called Terminal (search for it in Spotlight). On Windows, open PowerShell. Everything below gets typed there, not in your browser.

**Install the Supabase CLI:**

Mac users: If you already have Homebrew installed, use the first option. Windows users: use Scoop. Linux or Mac without Homebrew: use npm.

```bash
# Mac with Homebrew
brew install supabase/tap/supabase

# Windows with Scoop (recommended)
# Install Scoop first if you don't have it:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser
Invoke-RestMethod -Uri https://get.scoop.sh | Invoke-Expression
# Then install Supabase:
scoop bucket add supabase https://github.com/supabase/scoop-bucket.git
scoop install supabase

# Linux or Mac without Homebrew
npm install -g supabase
```

Verify it worked:
```bash
supabase --version
```

**Log In and Link:**
```bash
supabase login
supabase link --project-ref YOUR_PROJECT_REF
```
Replace `YOUR_PROJECT_REF` with the project ref from your credential tracker (Step 1).

**Create the Function:**
```bash
supabase functions new ingest-thought
```

Open `supabase/functions/ingest-thought/index.ts` and replace its entire contents with:

[View & Copy Code -- ingest-thought Edge Function TypeScript]

**Set Your Secrets:**
```bash
supabase secrets set OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your-openrouter-key-here
supabase secrets set SLACK_BOT_TOKEN=xoxb-your-slack-bot-token-here
supabase secrets set SLACK_CAPTURE_CHANNEL=C0your-channel-id-here
```

SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY are automatically available inside Edge Functions -- you don't need to set them.

**Deploy:**
```bash
supabase functions deploy ingest-thought --no-verify-jwt
```

Copy the Edge Function URL immediately after deployment! It looks like: `https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/ingest-thought`

## Step 8: Connect Slack to the Edge Function

1. Go to api.slack.com/apps -> select your Open Brain app
2. Left sidebar -> Event Subscriptions -> toggle Enable Events ON
3. Paste your Edge Function URL in the Request URL field
4. Wait for the green checkmark (Verified)
5. Under Subscribe to bot events, add both: `message.channels` and `message.groups`
6. Click Save Changes (reinstall if prompted)

**Warning:** You need both events. Slack treats public and private channels as separate entity types. Public channels fire `message.channels`, private channels fire `message.groups`. If you only add one, messages in the other channel type will silently fail -- no error, just nothing happens. Add both so you're covered regardless of how your capture channel is configured.

## Step 9: Test It

Go to your capture channel in Slack and type:

> Sarah mentioned she's thinking about leaving her job to start a consulting business

Wait 5-10 seconds. You should see a threaded reply:

> Captured as person_note -- career, consulting
> People: Sarah
> Action items: Check in with Sarah about consulting plans

Then open Supabase dashboard -> Table Editor -> thoughts. You should see one row with your message, an embedding, and metadata.

If that works, Part 1 is done. You have a working capture system.

---

# Part 2 -- Retrieval

## A Quick Note on Architecture

MCP servers can run two ways: locally on your computer, or hosted in the cloud.

The local approach means installing Node.js, building a TypeScript project, and running a server process on your machine. Every AI client you connect needs the full path to that server plus your database credentials pasted into a config file. If your laptop is closed, your brain is offline. If you switch computers, you set it up again.

We're not doing that.

Your capture system already runs on Supabase -- the Edge Function you deployed in Part 1 handles Slack messages without anything running on your computer. The MCP server works the same way. One more Edge Function, deployed to the same project, reachable from anywhere. Your AI clients connect with a URL. No build steps, no local dependencies, no credentials on your machine.

If you want to run locally -- maybe you're a developer who prefers that, or you want to customize beyond what Edge Functions allow -- the MCP TypeScript SDK with StdioServerTransport works great. The Supabase docs on deploying MCP servers cover both approaches. Everything below uses hosted.

## Step 10: Create an Access Key

Your MCP server will be a public URL. The Supabase project ref in that URL is random enough that nobody will stumble onto it, but let's close the gap entirely. You'll generate a simple access key that the server checks on every request. Takes 30 seconds.

In your terminal, generate a random key:

```bash
# Mac/Linux
openssl rand -hex 32

# Windows (PowerShell)
-join ((1..32) | ForEach-Object { '{0:x2}' -f (Get-Random -Maximum 256) })
```

Copy the output -- it'll look something like `a3f8b2c1d4e5...` (64 characters). Paste it into your credential tracker under MCP Access Key.

Set it as a Supabase secret:
```bash
supabase secrets set MCP_ACCESS_KEY=your-generated-key-here
```

## Step 11: Deploy the MCP Server

One Edge Function. Four tools: semantic search, browse recent thoughts, stats, and capture. Same deployment process as the capture function.

**Create the Function:**
```bash
supabase functions new open-brain-mcp
```

**Add Dependencies:**
Create `supabase/functions/open-brain-mcp/deno.json`:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "@hono/mcp": "npm:@hono/mcp@0.1.1",
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "npm:@modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.24.3",
    "hono": "npm:hono@4.9.2",
    "zod": "npm:zod@4.1.13",
    "@supabase/supabase-js": "npm:@supabase/supabase-js@2.47.10"
  }
}
```

**Write the Server:**
Open `supabase/functions/open-brain-mcp/index.ts` and replace its entire contents with:

[View & Copy Code -- MCP server Edge Function TypeScript]

**Deploy:**
```bash
supabase functions deploy open-brain-mcp --no-verify-jwt
```

Your MCP server is now live at:
`https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp`

Replace `YOUR_PROJECT_REF` with the project ref from your credential tracker (Step 1). Paste the full URL into your credential tracker as the MCP Server URL.

Now build your MCP Connection URL by adding your access key to the end:
`https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp?key=your-access-key-from-step-10`

Paste this into your credential tracker as the MCP Connection URL. This is what you'll give to AI clients that support remote MCP -- one URL, no extra config.

That's it. No npm install, no TypeScript build, no local server to keep running. It's deployed alongside your capture function and runs on Supabase's infrastructure.

## Step 12: Connect to Your AI

You need your MCP Connection URL from the credential tracker -- the one with `?key=` at the end.

### Claude Desktop

1. Open Claude Desktop -> Settings -> Connectors
2. Click Add custom connector
3. Name: Open Brain
4. Remote MCP server URL: paste your MCP Connection URL (the one ending in `?key=your-access-key`)
5. Click Add

That's it. Start a new conversation, and Claude will have access to your Open Brain tools. You can enable or disable it per conversation via the "+" button -> Connectors.

No JSON config files. No Node.js. No terminal. If you had trouble with earlier versions of this guide, this is the fix.

### ChatGPT

Requires a paid ChatGPT plan (Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise, or Edu) and works on the web at chatgpt.com. Not available on mobile.

**Enable Developer Mode (one-time setup):**
1. Go to chatgpt.com -> click your profile icon -> Settings
2. Navigate to Apps & Connectors -> Advanced settings
3. Toggle Developer mode ON

**Warning:** Enabling Developer Mode disables ChatGPT's built-in Memory feature. Yes, that's ironic for a brain tool. Your Open Brain replaces that functionality anyway -- and it works across every AI, not just ChatGPT.

**Add the connector:**
1. In Settings -> Apps & Connectors, click Create
2. Name: Open Brain
3. Description: Personal knowledge base with semantic search (or whatever you want -- this is just for your reference)
4. MCP endpoint URL: paste your MCP Connection URL (the one ending in `?key=your-access-key`)
5. Authentication: select No Authentication (your access key is embedded in the URL)
6. Click Create

**Using it:** Start a new conversation and make sure the Open Brain connector is enabled -- check the tools/apps panel at the top of the chat. ChatGPT is less intuitive than Claude at picking the right MCP tool automatically. If it doesn't use your brain on its own, be explicit: "Use the Open Brain search_thoughts tool to find my notes about project planning." After it gets the pattern once or twice in a conversation, it usually picks up the habit.

### Claude Code

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http open-brain \
  https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp \
  --header "x-brain-key: your-access-key-from-step-10"
```

### Other Clients (Cursor, VS Code Copilot, Windsurf)

Every MCP client handles remote servers slightly differently. The server accepts your access key two ways -- pick whichever your client supports:

**Option A: URL with key (easiest).** If your client has a field for a remote MCP server URL, paste the full MCP Connection URL including `?key=your-access-key`. This works for any client that supports remote MCP without requiring headers.

**Option B: mcp-remote bridge.** If your client only supports local stdio servers (configured via a JSON config file), use mcp-remote to bridge to the remote server. This requires Node.js installed.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "open-brain": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "mcp-remote",
        "https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/open-brain-mcp",
        "--header",
        "x-brain-key:${BRAIN_KEY}"
      ],
      "env": {
        "BRAIN_KEY": "your-access-key-from-step-10"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Note:** no space after the colon in `x-brain-key:${BRAIN_KEY}`. Some clients have a bug where spaces inside args get mangled.

## Step 13: Use It

Ask your AI naturally. It picks the right tool automatically:

| Prompt | Tool Used |
|--------|-----------|
| "What did I capture about career changes?" | Semantic search |
| "What did I capture this week?" | Browse recent |
| "How many thoughts do I have?" | Stats overview |
| "Find my notes about the API redesign" | Semantic search |
| "Show me my recent ideas" | Browse + filter |
| "Who do I mention most?" | Stats |
| "Save this: decided to move the launch to March 15 because of the QA blockers" | Capture thought |
| "Remember that Marcus wants to move to the platform team" | Capture thought |

The capture tool means you're not limited to Slack for input. Any MCP-connected AI can write directly to your brain -- Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, Claude Code, Cursor. Wherever you're working, you can save a thought without switching apps.

---

## Troubleshooting

If the specific suggestions below don't solve your issue, remember: the Supabase AI assistant (chat icon, bottom-right of your dashboard) can help diagnose problems with anything Supabase-related. Paste the error message and tell it what step you're on.

### Capture Issues (Part 1)

**Slack says "Request URL not verified":**
Your Edge Function isn't deployed or isn't reachable. Run the deploy command again and check the output for errors.
```bash
supabase functions deploy ingest-thought --no-verify-jwt
```

**Messages aren't triggering the function:**
Check Event Subscriptions -- make sure both `message.channels` and `message.groups` are listed (public channels use the first, private channels use the second -- you need both). Verify the app is invited to the channel. Confirm the channel ID in your secrets matches the actual channel.

**Slack creates duplicate database entries:**
Slack retries webhook delivery if it doesn't get a response within 3 seconds. If your Edge Function takes longer than that (embedding + metadata extraction can take 4-5 seconds), Slack sends the event again, and you get two rows. This is a known edge case. The captures are identical, so it doesn't affect search -- but if it bothers you, you can delete the duplicate row in the Supabase Table Editor.

**Function runs but nothing in the database:**
Check Edge Function logs: Supabase dashboard -> Edge Functions -> ingest-thought -> Logs. Most likely the OpenRouter key is wrong or has no credits.
```bash
supabase secrets list
```

**No confirmation reply in Slack:**
The bot token might be wrong, or `chat:write` scope wasn't added. Go to your Slack app -> OAuth & Permissions and verify. If you added the scope after installing, you need to reinstall the app.

**Metadata extraction seems off:**
That's normal -- the LLM is making its best guess with limited context. The metadata is a convenience layer on top of semantic search, not the primary retrieval mechanism. The embedding handles fuzzy matching regardless.

### Retrieval Issues (Part 2)

**Claude Desktop tools don't appear:**
Make sure you added the connector in Settings -> Connectors (not by editing the JSON config file). Verify the connector is enabled for your conversation -- click the "+" button at the bottom of the chat, then Connectors, and check that Open Brain is toggled on. If the connector was added but tools still don't show, try removing and re-adding it with the same URL.

**ChatGPT doesn't use the Open Brain tools:**
First, confirm Developer Mode is enabled (Settings -> Apps & Connectors -> Advanced settings). Without it, ChatGPT only exposes limited MCP functionality that won't cover Open Brain's full toolset. Next, check that the connector is active for your current conversation -- look for it in the tools/apps panel. If it's connected but ChatGPT ignores it, be direct: "Use the Open Brain search_thoughts tool to search for [topic]." ChatGPT often needs explicit tool references the first few times before it starts picking them up automatically.

**Getting 401 errors:**
The access key doesn't match what's stored in Supabase secrets. Double-check that the `?key=` value in your URL matches your MCP Access Key exactly. If you're using the header approach (Claude Code or mcp-remote), the header must be `x-brain-key` (lowercase, with the dash).

**Search returns no results:**
Make sure you sent test messages in Part 1 first. Try asking the AI to "search with threshold 0.3" for a wider net. If that still returns nothing, check the Edge Function logs in the Supabase dashboard for errors.

**Tools work but responses are slow:**
First search on a cold function takes a few seconds -- the Edge Function is waking up. Subsequent calls are faster. If it's consistently slow, check your Supabase project region -- pick the one closest to you.

**Capture tool saves but metadata is wrong:**
Same as Slack capture -- the metadata extraction is best-effort. The embedding is what powers semantic search, and that works regardless of how the metadata gets classified. If you consistently want a specific classification, use the capture templates from the prompt kit to give the LLM clearer signals.

---

## How It Works Under the Hood

**When you type a message in Slack:** Slack sends it to your Edge Function -> the function generates an embedding (1536-dimensional vector of meaning) AND extracts metadata via LLM in parallel -> both get stored as a single row in Supabase -> the function replies in your Slack thread with a summary.

**When you capture from any AI via MCP:** your AI client sends the text to the capture_thought tool -> the MCP server generates an embedding AND extracts metadata in parallel (same pipeline as Slack) -> stored as a single row -> confirmation returned to your AI.

**When you ask your AI about it:** your AI client sends the query to the MCP Edge Function -> the function generates an embedding of your question -> Supabase matches it against every stored thought by vector similarity -> results come back ranked by meaning, not keywords.

The embedding is what makes retrieval powerful. "Sarah's thinking about leaving" and "What did I note about career changes?" match semantically even though they share zero keywords. The metadata is a bonus layer for structured filtering on top.

## Swapping Models Later

Because you're using OpenRouter, you can swap models by editing the model strings in the Edge Function code and redeploying. Browse available models at openrouter.ai/models. Just make sure embedding dimensions match (1536 for the current setup).

## What You Just Built -- And What You Can Build Next

You just used three free services, some copy-pasted code, and a built-in AI assistant to build a personal knowledge system with semantic search, an open write protocol, and an open read protocol. No CS degree. No local servers. No monthly SaaS fee.

Here's the thing worth noticing: that Supabase AI assistant that helped you through the setup? It has access to all of Supabase's documentation, understands your project structure, and can help you build on top of what you've created. That's not a one-time trick for getting unstuck during setup. That's a permanent building partner.

Want to add a new capture source beyond Slack? Ask it how to create another Edge Function. Want to add a new field to your thoughts table? Ask it to help you write the SQL migration. Want to understand how to add authentication so you can share your brain with a teammate? It knows the docs better than you ever will.

You just built AI infrastructure using AI. That pattern doesn't stop here.

## Your Next Step

Your Open Brain is live. Now make it work for you. The companion prompt pack -- Open Brain: Companion Prompts -- covers the full lifecycle from here:

- **Memory Migration** -- Pull everything your AI already knows about you into your brain so every tool starts with context instead of zero
- **Second Brain Migration** -- Bring your existing notes from Notion, Obsidian, or any other system into your Open Brain without starting over
- **Open Brain Spark** -- Personalized use case discovery based on your actual workflow, not generic examples
- **Quick Capture Templates** -- Five patterns optimized for clean metadata extraction so your brain tags and retrieves accurately
- **The Weekly Review** -- A Friday ritual that surfaces themes, forgotten action items, and connections you missed

Start with the Memory Migration. If you have an existing second brain, run the Second Brain Migration next. Then use the Spark to figure out what to capture going forward. The templates build the daily habit. The weekly review closes the loop.

---

Built by Nate B. Jones -- companion to "Your Second Brain Is Closed. Your AI Can't Use It. Here's the Fix."

---

**Important note:** This is the "Open Brain" system by Nate B. Jones, not Steve Yegge's "beads" system. The guide describes a 13-step setup for a personal knowledge system using Supabase, OpenRouter, and Slack, with MCP-based retrieval for AI tools. Several code blocks (the credential tracker template, the thoughts table SQL, the match_thoughts function SQL, and the two Edge Function TypeScript files for `ingest-thought` and `open-brain-mcp`) are behind interactive "View & Copy Code" buttons on the live page and were not captured in this text extraction. You would need to visit the page directly to expand and copy those code blocks.
